weekly_brief_draft:
## Lead Signal

No new foreign information manipulation and interference operations were identified in the research window of April 22-28, 2026. Exhaustive search across Tier 1-3 sources yielded no new FIMI disclosures, platform takedowns, or government attributions published during this period. Most recent activity predates the research window, with the latest notable disclosures from Google Threat Analysis Group bulletins dated January 29, 2026, covering Q4 2025 activities.

This absence of new signals underscores the structural OSINT gaps in tracking FIMI campaigns, where public disclosures from platforms and governments remain episodic rather than continuous. The information integrity composite score remains stable at 0.6, reflecting steady but incomplete attribution capacity, platform transparency, regulatory coverage, enforcement capacity, and cross-actor parity. No shifts in these components were observed this week.

## Other Developments

**Actor postures remain stable across tracked entities.** The actor tracker in module 3 reports no changes for Russia, China, Iran, Gulf states, United States, and Israel. All maintain status as stable with postures of no new operations disclosed this week. For instance, Russia-linked operations targeting Moldova with Romanian-language content on YouTube, as reported in Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin, saw no follow-on disclosures. China DRAGONBRIDGE remains the most prolific IO actor tracked by Google TAG, but no new takedowns occurred in the window. Similar stability holds for other actors, with EEAS coverage varying from tracked to not tracked.

**Platform posture scorecard shows no movement.** Meta, Google/YouTube maintain leading transparency postures with high disclosure cadence and proactive DSA compliance. X/Twitter, TikTok, and Telegram remain opaque or lagging with low cadence and resistant compliance. No new FIMI operations prompted enforcement actions or transparency reports from any platform this week.

**Regulatory and policy landscape unchanged.** No new regulatory or policy developments disclosed this week. EU DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions implementation status shows no updates, remaining active with last material change on 2026-05-02. EEAS RAS FIMI Toolbox similarly active with no new developments.

**Risk matrix confirms stable trajectories.** Actor risk matrix rates Russia and China at high overall risk with high operational tempo, attribution confidence, and platform reach. Iran, Gulf, US, and Israel at moderate risk. All trajectories stable with no new operations disclosed.

## Cross-Monitor Connections

This week null cycle aligns with continuity across sibling monitors. Democratic-integrity monitor likely observes stable electoral narratives absent new FIMI inputs. Conflict-escalation signals in SCEM information warfare theaters show no amplification from fresh FIMI campaigns. European-strategic-autonomy ESA hybrid threats and DSA enforcement remain steady without platform takedown crossovers. Macro-monitor economic coercion narratives and ai-governance AGM AI-enabled FIMI tools report no intersecting developments. Environmental-risks ERM climate disinformation vectors unchanged. The absence of material FIMI change reinforces baseline continuity rather than escalation in connected domains.

## Outlook

Next week will monitor for any delayed disclosures from Tier 1 sources such as Google TAG, Meta, or EEAS RAS updates that may post-date the research window. Stability in actor postures and platform scorecards persists unless new takedowns or attributions emerge. Watch for potential carryover from Q4 2025 patterns, such as Russia Moldova targeting or China DRAGONBRIDGE amplification, if platforms release quarterly summaries. Gaps in OSINT coverage for undertracked actors like Gulf states and US warrant attention to any government statements or civil society reports that could elevate possible claims to assessed confidence.
